title: Design a URL Shortener
overview: Build a service like bit.ly that takes a long URL and returns a short alias. Users visit the short URL and get redirected to the original.

levels.title: Basic Shortener
levels.constraints: 10K URLs created per day. Single region. Reads and writes are roughly equal. Design the core shorten + redirect flow.
levels.feedbackHint: Solid start! At high read volume, every redirect hitting the DB will become a bottleneck.
levels.aiContextHint: Level 1 is a basic monolithic design. A client posts a URL, the server hashes it, stores the mapping in a relational DB, and returns the short code. Accept any design with a server + database.

levels.title: High Read Traffic
levels.constraints: 100M redirects per day, but only 1M new URLs created. Read:write = 100:1. DB reads are timing out under load.
levels.feedbackHint: Caching short-code lookups dramatically reduces DB load. Next: how do you serve users globally with low latency?
levels.aiContextHint: Level 2 requires a cache (Redis/Memcached) to serve redirects without hitting the DB on every request. The API Gateway handles rate limiting and routing.

levels.title: Global Scale
levels.constraints: Users worldwide. Redirects must complete in < 50ms. The server is in one region — latency to other continents is 200ms+.
levels.feedbackHint: Excellent! A CDN caches redirect responses at edge nodes globally, eliminating origin round trips for popular URLs.
levels.aiContextHint: Level 3 adds a CDN to cache redirect responses at edge locations. Popular short URLs never hit the origin server — the CDN handles the redirect directly.
